_comment: Absolute performance budgets — the single source of truth for all three perf scopes (DB-layer runner, k6 HTTP, BenchmarkDotNet). A scope exits non-zero when a measured value breaches its budget. Most budgets are calibrated from a 1M-row run on a dev machine (2026-06); RECALIBRATE on your target/CI hardware (set each ~20-30% above the observed p95/mean). A missing key = measure but never fail.
_comment_stats: The statistics aggregates (statsSummary/statsLatencyPercentiles/statsTokenUsage/statsModelBreakdown/statsCostEstimate/statsCallTrends) translate to server-side GROUP BY/sum/percentile_cont — they do NOT client-evaluate. Their cost is one scan of the windowed rows: sub-second at 1M after planner statistics are current. The ~3.7-4.4s seen on the first 1M run (issue #246) was a stale-statistics artifact — a freshly bulk-seeded table with no ANALYZE made the planner pick a nested-loop plan that random-read the whole table; the same SQL runs ~270-880ms once analyzed (3452ms -> 478ms on EXPLAIN). The seeder now ANALYZEs after the bulk load and migration TuneAgentCallAutovacuum keeps stats fresh in production, so these budgets are real measured p95 + ~30-45% headroom on a 1M dev run (2026-06). The three heaviest (latencyPercentiles ~880ms full sort, tokenUsage/callTrends ~780-880ms bucketed full scans) are scan-bound — no index helps a full-window aggregate; sub-second at 10M+ would need pre-aggregated rollups. RECALIBRATE on your CI hardware (~20-30% above observed p95). statsPulse is the exception to the scan-bound rule: a trailing-60-minute window on indexed CreatedAt resolves to an index range scan (~1.6ms p95 on a fresh 1M seed). It used to be history-dependent on kept-DB iterate runs: the db-layer ingest probe left its 5k freshly-timestamped rows behind and those were the only rows landing in the trailing-hour window, so statsPulse climbed ~0.7ms per 1k accumulated probe rows (1.6 -> 5.2 -> 8.1 -> 12.5 -> 19.5ms over five back-to-back runs) until they aged out after 60min. Issue #294 fixed that at the source: the ingest probe now deletes exactly the rows it inserted after recording throughput (IngestionThroughputScenario.CleanupProbeRowsAsync, outside the timed section so the ingestThroughput number is untouched), restoring the post-seed state, so statsPulse is stable across iterate runs (verified 2026-07 at a 100k seed: the AgentCallEntity row count returns to baseline after every run and p95 stays ~1.5ms run-over-run instead of climbing). The 25ms budget is now conservative — it no longer needs headroom for probe accumulation and CAN be tightened to the fresh-seed p95 + ~30-45% (single-digit ms) once re-measured on a full 1M seed (DEFERRED: the fix was verified at 100k, not re-measured at 1M, so the number is left untouched rather than invented). It still sits far below the regression signature — losing the CreatedAt index means a full scan, 250ms+.
_comment_proxyResolve: proxyResolveProxytraceKey/proxyResolveUpstreamKey guard the proxy's per-request credential resolution (issue #407). The proxy deliberately resolves credentials from storage on EVERY proxied request — no positive cache, so key rotation/revocation takes effect on the next request — which makes this a hot per-request path that must stay flat as data grows: a handful of indexed point lookups (ApiKey.KeyHash unique index / ModelProvider.ApiKeyLookupHash blind index / PK hydrations + one Data Protection decrypt), independent of AgentCall volume. Each iteration runs in a fresh lifetime scope because per-request scope/DbContext construction is part of the cost the proxy pays. Both budgets are UNCALIBRATED PLACEHOLDERS (set conservatively; expected single-digit ms) — RECALIBRATE ~20-30% above observed p95 on a full 1M run. A breach signals a regression that made resolution scale with table size (a lost index, an accidental join to a high-volume table) or a per-request storage path that got heavier.
_comment_anomaly: anomalyTimeline guards the anomaly-dashboard timeline aggregate (GetAnomalyCountsByAgentAsync): a GROUP BY (bucket, agent) with bitmask split counts over WHERE OutlierFlags <> 0, which rides the partial outlier index — the seeder flags ~3% of calls, so at 1M rows the scan touches ~30k rows, far cheaper than the full-window stats aggregates. Recalibrated 2026-07-03 after the traces-sorting columns widened the row (TotalTokens/CacheHitRate): observed p95 54.8ms warm, 64.8ms on the first cold pass after a fresh seed — the budget covers the cold pass.
_comment_sessions: agentCallsListBySession/sessionsRecent guard the trace-sessions feature (project-owned debugging groupings). agentCallsListBySession is the session-detail page's traces list: WHERE SessionId = @id ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC LIMIT 50 + the page COUNT, both served by the (SessionId, CreatedAt) index — an index range read over one session's slice, independent of total table size, so it sits in-family with the other narrowly-filtered list queries (agentCallsListByAgent, budget 40). sessionsRecent is the recent-sessions list: WHERE ProjectId = @p ORDER BY LastActivityAt DESC LIMIT 50 + a project COUNT over the Sessions table, served by the descending (ProjectId, LastActivityAt) index — it never aggregates the high-volume traces table because TraceCount/TotalTokens are denormalized onto the Sessions row at ingestion (RecordActivityAsync). The seeder tags ~30% of calls with a SessionId drawn from a ~TargetCalls*0.30/3 pool and inserts a matching Sessions row per pool entry (denormalized counters accumulated over its member calls), so both queries measure against realistic cardinality. Budgets calibrated 2026-07-17 at 1M calls / ~100k sessions: both p95 sub-millisecond index reads; the 40ms budget is small-number/CI-jitter headroom, in-family with the fast list queries (same as agentCallToolNamesByAgent). RECALIBRATE on your CI hardware. The ingestion budget (callsPerSecMin 150) is unchanged: ~30% of the throughput probe's calls now also pay the session upsert (RecordActivityAsync), matching production — if throughput regresses below 150 the upsert needs work, do NOT lower the budget.
_comment_traceSort: agentCallsListSort* guard the traces table's server-side column sorts (SortBy whitelist) measured at their worst case: no narrowing filter, whole-table top-50 at 1M rows. The nullable columns (LatencyMs/TotalTokens/CacheHitRate) sort with a '(col IS NULL)' pre-key for nulls-last-in-both-directions, which the planner cannot serve from the plain single-column index — the plan is a parallel seq scan + top-N heapsort (~230ms of the total; the rest is the page COUNT). ToolCount rides its index via incremental sort but its low cardinality makes that no cheaper. Filtered sorts (project/agent/time window — the normal UI state) are far cheaper. If these ever blow past budget, the lever is a raw-SQL '(col DESC NULLS LAST, Id DESC)' index pair per column plus NULLS LAST ordering EF can't express — deliberately not paid while worst case sits ~300-440ms. agentCallsListByToolName guards the ToolName EXISTS semi-join against AgentCallToolEntity ((ToolName, AgentCallId) index); agentCallToolNames guards the project-scoped DISTINCT picker ((ProjectId, ToolName) index-only scan); agentCallToolNamesByAgent guards the agent-scoped picker shown when an agent filter is active — AgentId is denormalised onto the tool row so it stays a single-table index-only DISTINCT over the (ProjectId, AgentId, ToolName) index (no join to the call table), and being scoped to one agent it scans only that agent's slice of the index, so it is far cheaper than the project-wide picker (~2ms vs ~67ms measured). Calibrated 2026-07-03 at 1M calls / ~600k tool rows: sorts p95 310-440ms, byToolName ~297ms, toolNames ~72ms; agentCallToolNamesByAgent recalibrated 2026-07-04 at 1M calls (p95 ~2.2ms, budget 40ms for small-number/CI jitter headroom, in-family with the fast list queries).
_comment_testRunStats: testRunStatsBySuite/testRunStatsBySuitePage guard the suite-scoped TestRunStats projection query (issue #253). The test-suites controller previously materialized the whole TestRunStatsEntity table and filtered in memory on every suites list + single-suite GET; the fix pushes the suite set into SQL (WHERE SuiteId IN (...)), backed by the existing index on TestRunStatsEntity.SuiteId. The query returns only the rows for the requested suite(s), so it is an index range read independent of total table size. testRunStatsPassTotals/testRunStatsRecentCohorts guard the dashboard's server-side TestRunStats aggregates (issue #288): the pass-rate totals (single scalar GROUP BY row) and the sparkline cohorts (GROUP BY (GroupId, EndpointId) ordered by max(RunCompletedAt), capped at 50) that replaced two O(all-history) materializations in DashboardStatistics. All four budgets are UNCALIBRATED PLACEHOLDERS (set conservatively; the aggregates scan the ~25k-row seeded table but return a bounded result) — RECALIBRATE on a full ~1M / 25k-test-run seed (set ~20-30% above the observed p95).
